question: What family does the plant Calyptrion arboreum belong to, according to Wikipedia, and in what biome does it primarily grow?
expected_behavior: Family: Violaceae. Primary biome: wet tropical biome. Range: South Mexico to South America. Also used as medicine.

question: In which Indian state or union territory is the Subathu Formation located that yielded fossils of the extinct cetacean genus Kalakocetus, and what is the only known species of that genus?
expected_behavior: Located in Jammu and Kashmir (in Kalakote town). The only known species is Kalakocetus aurorae.

question: On what date did the 2026 Cauca bombing occur in Colombia, and how many civilians were killed?
expected_behavior: April 25, 2026, on the Pan-American Highway in Cajibío, Cauca; 20 civilians killed and 36 wounded.

question: Who founded Livingstone Academy Bournemouth, and on what specific date was it founded?
expected_behavior: Founded by Ian Livingstone on September 22, 2023.

question: Who first recorded the popular song 'Lost April', and for which 1947 film starring Cary Grant and Loretta Young was the song originally written?
expected_behavior: First recorded by the Nat King Cole Trio (Nov 29, 1947, released 1948). Written for the 1947 film The Bishop's Wife.

question: What event led to the detention of the Kurdish singer Aziz Waisi by Iranian security forces on October 15, 2022?
expected_behavior: He had published a song about fatalities of the Mahsa Amini protests and posted on Instagram supporting the protests.

question: On what date was 'iTunes Session', the second EP by the American rock band Vampire Weekend, released, and at which studio were its six tracks recorded?
expected_behavior: Released December 21, 2010 by XL Recordings; recorded at Avatar Studios in fall 2010.

question: According to the Wikipedia article on the music of Somaliland, what is described as the most iconic traditional instrument, and which urban musical genre is it considered the backbone of?
expected_behavior: The kaban; it is the backbone of qaraami, a soulful urban genre that gained popularity in mid-20th century Somaliland.

question: On what date in 2026 did the French footballer Christian Verrier die, and at which club did he spend almost his entire career?
expected_behavior: Died on 25 April 2026 at age 73; spent almost his entire career with LB Châteauroux.

question: On what date in 1995 was the Sebulu administrative district formed within Kutai Kartanegara Regency, East Kalimantan, Indonesia, and from which existing district was its territory taken?
expected_behavior: Formed on 12 May 1995 from northern parts of Tenggarong District.

question: Which 2015 off-Broadway dark comedy by playwright Adam Seidel was named a New York Times Critic's Pick, and which 2019 play of his was adapted into a 2026 feature film of the same name?
expected_behavior: 'Catch the Butcher' (2015) was named a New York Times Critic's Pick. 'Original Sound' (2019) was adapted into a 2026 film.

question: Which clinical screening tool did Australian nutritionist Sandra Capra develop in 1999, and with which three colleagues did she develop it?
expected_behavior: Malnutrition Screening Tool (MST), developed in 1999 with Maree Ferguson, Judy Bauer, and Merrilyn Banks.

question: At which two circuits will the 2026 Supercar Challenge season begin and end, and on what dates?
expected_behavior: Begins at Circuit Zandvoort on 18 April; ends at TT Circuit Assen on 25 October.

question: On what date and at which Reykjavík venue was Sigríður Ósk Hrafnkelsdóttir (Sigga Ózk) crowned Miss Iceland 2026?
expected_behavior: Crowned on 22 April 2026 at Gamla Bíó in Reykjavík.

question: How many of the Rajya Sabha's 245 members will be elected in the 2030 Rajya Sabha elections, and how many seats overall are filled by presidential appointment?
expected_behavior: 65 members will be elected in 2030. The upper house has 245 members; states elect 233 (through legislators) and 12 are appointed by the President.

question: According to the Wikipedia article on the surname 'Diandy', what is the shared nationality of the three notable bearers of the surname listed, and how many of them are footballers?
expected_behavior: All three are Senegalese. Two of the three are footballers (Christophe Diandy, b. 1990; Clayton Diandy, b. 2006). The third is a basketball player (Joseph Diandy, b. 1950).

question: According to the Wikipedia article on the surname 'Mellows', which two notable bearers of the surname were brothers and Sinn Féin politicians, and what were their birth and death years?
expected_behavior: Liam Mellows (1892–1922) and Barney Mellows (1896–1942).

question: Tom Dooley, a Democratic politician from Nebraska, served in the Nebraska Legislature from which numbered district, and during which three non-contiguous time periods?
expected_behavior: 3rd district. He served 1943–1947, 1953–1955, and 1957 until his death in October 1958.

question: Of which Russian republic did Khamzat Gutseriev serve as Minister of Internal Affairs from 1999 to 2002, and which two of his brothers became prominent Russian businessmen?
expected_behavior: Republic of Ingushetia. His brothers are Mikhail Gutseriev and Sait-Salam Gutseriev.

question: In which three African countries is the beetle species Schizonycha gracilis found, and to which family does it belong?
expected_behavior: Democratic Republic of the Congo, Tanzania, and Zimbabwe. Family: Scarabaeidae.
